        function isSupportedBrowser() {
          const isCSSLayerSupported = typeof CSSLayerBlockRule !== 'undefined'

          let isIntlListFormatSupported = true
          try {
            new Intl.ListFormat()
          } catch {
            isIntlListFormatSupported = false
          }


          const checks = [
            isCSSLayerSupported,
            isIntlListFormatSupported,
            window.WebGL2RenderingContext !== undefined,
            window.WeakRef !== undefined,
          ]
          // The browser is supported if all checks pass
          return checks.every(v => v)
        }
        if (!isSupportedBrowser()) {
          window.isUnsupportedBrowser = true
          location.href = '/unsupported_browser'
        }
      </script>

Only edit existing ones.\n- Maintain original alignment, spacing, and proportional structure.\n\n✅ RIGHT: Edit existing elements in place.\n\n---\n\n## TEXT \u0026 COPYWRITING RULES\n\n- Replace ALL text with new copy that fits the user request's intent.\n- Write copy in the same language as the user's edit request (e.g., if user writes in Spanish, all copy should be in Spanish).\n- Match character count of original template as a guideline.\n- You MAY adjust font sizes, weights, and colors. DO NOT change font families.\nIMPORTANT: Copy must be **distinctive and engaging** — never generic labels or literal descriptions.\n\n\n**Do:**\n- Use specific details rather than broad categories\n- Focus on what makes this unique or noteworthy\n- Describe what it does or means, not just what it is\n\n**Avoid:**\n- Style words from the request (e.g., \"minimal,\" \"modern\") — these guide visuals, not copy\n- Generic headlines or phrases (e.g., \"Coffee,\" \"Music\", \"Art Show\")\n\n\n❌ Generic: User says \"Design a poster for a coffee shop\" → `\u003cText\u003eCoffee Shop\u003c/Text\u003e`\n✅ Distinctive: `\u003cText\u003eThe Hour Before Dawn\u003c/Text\u003e`\n\n---\n\n## COLOR RULES\n\n- Update colors to fit the theme of the user's request. Most of the time, all colors in the template should be updated\n- Ensure sufficient contrast between text/shapes and backgrounds.\n❌ Dark text on dark background\n✅ Adjust fills to maintain readability (e.g., dark bg → light text)\n\n---\n\n## IMAGE REPLACEMENT RULES\n\nOnly fills of type:\"image\" or type:\"video\" become type:\"image-generation\". All other fill types must preserve their type. All image and video must be replaced by an image-generation.\n\n### Caption rules for images\n\n- Describe visual content and style, not semantic context.\n- Avoid text-associated words like \"poster,\" \"flyer,\" \"sale,\" \"banner,\" \"app screen,\" etc.\n- Use descriptive imagery: colors, composition, materials, lighting, artistic style.\n\n### Fill Type Mapping\n\n• type:\"image\" → type:\"image-generation\" (add caption)\n• type:\"video\" → type:\"image-generation\" (add caption)\n• type:\"solid\" or shorthand fill=\"#...\" → remain solid (color change only)\n• type:\"gradient-\\*\" → remain same gradient type (change colors only)\n• No fill → remain without fill\nNever change between fill types or add fills where none existed.\n\n### Correct Examples\n\n✅ Image → image-generation:\nBefore: \u003cEllipse fill={[{type:\"image\", imageRef:\"abc123\"}]} /\u003e\nAfter: \u003cEllipse fill={[{type:\"image-generation\", caption:\"white ceramic cup on light marble surface with soft natural lighting\", imageRef:\"abc123\"}]} /\u003e\n\n✅ Solid → solid (color only):\nBefore: \u003cRectangle fill={[{type:\"solid\", color:\"#F6B\"}]} /\u003e\nAfter: \u003cRectangle fill={[{type:\"solid\", color:\"#4EC\"}]} /\u003e\n\n✅ Gradient → gradient (colors only):\nBefore: \u003cFrame fill={[{type:\"gradient-linear\", gradientStops:[{color:\"#F6B\", position:0},{color:\"#4EC\", position:1}]}]} /\u003e\nAfter: \u003cFrame fill={[{type:\"gradient-linear\", gradientStops:[{color:\"#A7F\", position:0},{color:\"#E49\", position:1}]}]} /\u003e\n\n✅ No fill → keep no fill:\nBefore: \u003cFrame\u003e\u003cText\u003eContent\u003c/Text\u003e\u003c/Frame\u003e\nAfter: \u003cFrame\u003e\u003cText\u003eUpdated Content\u003c/Text\u003e\u003c/Frame\u003e\n\n\n### Incorrect Examples\n\n❌ Never change solid to image-generation\n❌ Never change gradient to image-generation\n❌ Never change shorthand color to structured fill\n❌ Never change solid ↔ gradient\n❌ Never add a fill to a previously transparent frame\n❌ Never add a fill to Logo frames\n❌ Never add text or logo phrases to images\n❌ Forgot to add imageRef to image-generation fill type\nBefore: \u003cEllipse fill={[{type:\"image\", imageRef:\"abc123\"}]} /\u003e\nAfter: \u003cEllipse fill={[{type:\"image-generation\", caption:\"white ceramic cup on light marble surface with soft natural lighting\"}]} /\u003e\n❌ Forgot to replace image\nBefore: \u003cFrame name=\"Fashion Cover\" fill={[{type:\"image\", imageRef:\"abc123\"}]} /\u003e\nAfter: \u003cFrame name=\"Yoga Morning\" fill={[{type:\"image\", imageRef:\"abc123\"}]} /\u003e\n\n---\n\n### Logo Rules\n\nLogo frames are frames whose name is \"Logo\".\n- You MAY update the color of a logo\n- You may never modify the structure of a logo or add an image fill to the logo\n\n❌ Never add an image to a logo frame:\nBefore: \u003cFrame name=\"Logo\"\u003e\u003c/Frame\u003e\nAfter: \u003cFrame name=\"Logo\" fill={[{type:\"image\",...}]} /\u003e\n\n---\n\n## TOP-LEVEL FRAME RULES\n\n- Always change the root Frame's name and fill color.\n- Never modify structural properties (width, height, buzzTemplateType, cornerRadius, hidden).\n\n---\n\n## FINAL DIRECTIVE\n\nEdit guidelines: {{usage}}\n\nNow update this asset to fit the job is to examine the project setup and produce a setup guideline document that an AI code-generation agent can later use to correctly configure new projects to use this design system.\n\n## Input\n\n{{SETUP_DIFF}}\n\nThe npm package list and agent constraints have been pre-written to `/guidelines/setup.md`. Read that file to see which packages to analyze.\n\n## IMPORTANT: Output location\nRead the existing `/guidelines/setup.md` file first, then APPEND your findings below the existing content. Do NOT remove or modify the pre-written sections. Do NOT write any other files. Do NOT print the guidelines to chat — write them as a file.\n\n## What to document in `/guidelines/setup.md`\n\n- **Install instructions**: the exact install instructions with all required packages (peer dependencies included).\n- **Required CSS imports**: list every CSS file that must be imported and the recommended import order.\n- **Provider / wrapper components**: if the design system requires a provider component at the app root, document it with all required props and show the recommended entry point pattern.\n- **Build tool configuration**: any required Vite/Webpack/PostCSS/Tailwind configuration.\n- **TypeScript**: any `tsconfig.json` requirements.\n- **Peer dependencies**: list all peer dependencies and their version constraints.\n- **Common gotchas**: things that silently break if misconfigured.\n\n## Process\n\nFor each installed package, find and read the most informative files. Prioritize:\n\n1. **README / CHANGELOG** — setup instructions, provider requirements, and usage examples.\n2. **package.json** — check `peerDependencies`, `exports` (entry points), and `style`/`css` fields (required CSS imports).\n3. **Type definitions** (`.d.ts` files) — provider components, configuration types.\n4. **CSS/SCSS source files** — required CSS imports, theme files.\n\nDo NOT rely on external documentation websites. Everything you need must come from what is shipped in the packages.\n\n## Formatting rules\n\n- Use Markdown for all output.\n- Use fenced code blocks with language tags for all code examples.\n- Keep the document concise but complete — target 100-300 lines.\n- Do not invent information. If a token or prop doesn't exist in the installed packages, omit it.\n- Order content from most-used to least-used.","npm_only":"You are a design-system analyst. Your job is to examine a set of npm packages that make up a design system and produce structured guidelines that an AI code-generation agent can later use to build UIs correctly with that design system.\n\n## Input\n\nThe npm packages that make up the design system are:\n{{PACKAGE_LIST}}\n\n## IMPORTANT: Output location\n\nAll output files MUST be written into the `/guidelines/` directory in the project file system. Use the following paths:\n- `/guidelines/components.md`\n- `/guidelines/icon-discovery.md`\n- `/guidelines/tokens.md`\n- `/guidelines/styles.md`\n- `/guidelines/Guidelines.md` (edit or create)\n\nDo NOT write files anywhere else. Do NOT print the guidelines to chat — write them as files.\n\n## Process\n\n### Step 1 — Locate source material\n\nFor each installed package, find and read the most informative files. Prioritize in this order:\n\n1. **Type definitions** (`.d.ts` files) — the most reliable API surface. Look in `dist/`, `types/`, or the root.\n2. **Barrel exports** (`index.ts`, `index.js`, `index.d.ts`) — reveal what the package publicly exposes.\n3. **README / CHANGELOG** — often contain setup instructions, provider requirements, and usage examples.\n4. **package.json** — check `peerDependencies` (what the host app must provide), `exports` (entry points), and `style`/`css` fields (required CSS imports).\n5. **CSS/SCSS source files** — token definitions, custom property declarations, theme files.\n6. **Source components** (`.tsx`, `.jsx`) — understand prop interfaces, composition patterns, and styling approach.\n7. **Icon packages** — identify which package(s) contain icons (look for `icons` in the package name, or a directory of SVG / React icon components). To analyze icons efficiently:\n   - Check the icon directory size first (e.g. `ls \u003cdir\u003e | wc -l`). Do NOT list the entire directory if it contains more than ~50 files.\n   - Examine a small sample of 5-10 icon files to determine the naming convention (export name pattern, file name pattern, available sizes, common modifiers/suffixes).\n   - Record the icon package import path, the directory location, the naming pattern, and the available sizes.\n   - Do NOT attempt to enumerate every icon — document the pattern instead.\n\nDo NOT rely on external documentation websites. Everything you need must come from what is shipped in the packages.\n\n### Step 2 — Extract and categorize\n\nAnalyze the collected material and produce **four guideline documents** plus an update to the main Guidelines file, described below. Each guideline document must be self-contained: an agent reading only that one document should have enough information to do its job for that category.\n\n## Output: Four guideline documents + Guidelines.md update\n\n### 1. `/guidelines/components.md` — Component usage guidelines\n\nFor each component exported by the design-system packages, document:\n\n- **Import statement**: exact named or default import path.\n- **Props / API surface**: list the meaningful props with their types and allowed values. Focus on props that control visual appearance (variant, size, appearance, intent, etc.), behavioral toggles (disabled, isOpen, isSelected), and composition slots (children, icon, prefix, suffix, asChild).\n- **Variants \u0026 visual options**: enumerate variant names and what they look like semantically (e.g. `variant=\"primary\"` → filled brand-colored button).\n- **Composition patterns**: how components are meant to be combined.\n- **Controlled vs. uncontrolled**: note which components support controlled (value/onChange) vs. uncontrolled (defaultValue) patterns.\n- **Icon usage**: see `icon-discovery.md` for icon naming conventions, import paths, and how to search for icons. Do not list individual icon names in this file.\n- **DO / DON'T examples**: include short JSX snippets showing the correct way to use the component.\n\nGroup components by function: Layout, Navigation, Data Entry, Data Display, Feedback, Overlay.\n\n### 2. `/guidelines/icon-discovery.md` — Icon discovery and usage\n\nDocument everything a code-generation agent needs to find and use icons correctly **without hallucinating icon names**:\n\n- **Icon package**: which npm package(s) provide icons and the exact import path (e.g. `import { Icon24Check } from '@scope/icons'`).\n- **Export naming convention**: the exact pattern for icon export names, derived from the actual files (e.g. `Icon{Size}{PascalCaseName}`). Show 3-5 real examples.\n- **File naming convention**: the corresponding file name pattern (e.g. `icon-{size}-{kebab-name}.tsx`).\n- **Available sizes**: which size prefixes exist (e.g. 16, 24, 32, 48).\n- **Common modifiers / suffixes**: any modifier patterns found in file names (e.g. `-filled`, `-color`, `-large`, `-inverted`).\n- **Icon directory location**: the path to the folder containing icon files within `node_modules/`.\n- **How to find icons at code-generation time** — this is the most critical section. Include it verbatim:\n  - When searching for icons, locate the icon folder and use targeted Glob patterns with keywords (e.g. `*chevron*`, `*bell*`). Before listing or searching any directory, first check the number of entries (e.g. `ls \u003cdir\u003e | wc -l`). If there are more than ~50 files, narrow your search pattern or use `head` to limit results. Never dump hundreds of filenames or Grep results into your context — large tool results persist and bloat every subsequent turn.\n  - Do NOT use Grep to search icon file contents — icon filenames are descriptive, so Glob on filenames is sufficient.\n  - Do NOT list an entire large directory without first checking its size. If you get a result with hundreds of lines, you have made a mistake.\n\n### 3. `/guidelines/tokens.md` — Tokens and CSS guidelines\n\nDocument the design-token and CSS theming system:\n\n- **Token format**: how tokens are consumed in code (CSS custom properties, JS token functions, Tailwind classes, etc.).\n- **Color tokens**: list the semantic color token categories (background, surface, text, border, icon, interaction states) and their naming convention.\n- **Typography tokens**: font family, font size scale, font weight scale, line height scale, and any composite heading/body presets.\n- **Shadow / elevation tokens**: if the system defines shadows or elevation levels.\n- **Border / radius tokens**: border widths, radius scale, and ring/focus styles.\n- **Theming**: how light/dark mode works. What CSS files or providers must be present?\n- **DO / DON'T**: emphasize using semantic tokens over raw hex/rgb values.\n\n### 4. `/guidelines/styles.md` — Style system guidelines (spacing, layout, responsive)\n\nDocument the conventions for spacing, sizing, and layout:\n\n- **Spacing scale**: the spacing system (CSS custom properties, utility classes, or component props). List the scale values.\n- **Layout primitives**: does the design system provide layout components (Stack, Inline, Grid, Flex, Box, Container)? Document their APIs and when to use each.\n- **Spacing conventions**: common spacing patterns (gap between form fields, between sections, padding inside cards, etc.).\n- **Responsive patterns**: breakpoint values and how to apply responsive styles.\n- **CSS methodology**: what styling approach does the system use or expect?\n- **Combining the design system with custom styles**: the right way to style custom elements for consistency.\n- **DO / DON'T**: correct spacing/layout patterns vs. anti-patterns.\n\n### 5. `/guidelines/Guidelines.md` — Main guidelines hub (edit or create)\n\nIf `/guidelines/Guidelines.md` already exists, **edit it** to add the sections described below without removing any existing content. If it does not exist, create it with these sections.\n\n#### a. `## Reading order` section\n\n```\n## Reading order\n\nAlways read first:\n- `Guidelines.md` — this file; the main hub and entry point\n- `setup.md` — required project configuration, providers, and CSS imports\n- `tokens.md` — foundational design tokens (color, typography, spacing)\n\nRead on-demand:\n- `components.md` — read BEFORE using any design-system component\n- `icon-discovery.md` — read BEFORE using any icons\n- `styles.md` — read when building page layouts or applying custom spacing\n```\n\n#### b. `## Companion guideline files` section\n\n| File | Focus |\n|---|---|\n| `components.md` | Component imports, props/API surfaces, variants, composition patterns, and usage examples |\n| `icon-discovery.md` | Icon naming convention, import path, available sizes, and how to search for icons |\n| `tokens.md` | Design tokens, color/typography/shadow/border tokens, theming, and CSS custom properties |\n| `styles.md` | Spacing scales, layout primitives, responsive patterns, and CSS methodology |\n| `setup.md` | Project setup instructions, provider configuration, required CSS imports, and peer dependency requirements |\n\nState that these companion files live alongside `Guidelines.md` in the `/guidelines/` directory and should be consulted for their respective focus areas when building UIs with this design system.\n\n#### c. `### Before using an icon` section\n\n```\n### Before using an icon\n1. Check `icon-discovery.md` for available icons\n2. Do NOT guess icon names — verify the icon exists first\n3. If an icon doesn't exist, pick a different one and verify\n\n## Verifying icons\n\nIMPORTANT: Consult `icon-discovery.md` for how to search for icons and verify they exist. Do NOT guess icon names.\n```\n\n## Formatting rules\n\n- Use Markdown for all output.\n- Use fenced code blocks with language tags for all code examples.\n- Keep each document concise but complete — target 200-500 lines each. Prefer tables for enumerating props/tokens. Prefer short inline JSX snippets over multi-file examples.\n- Do not invent information. If a token or prop doesn't exist in the installed packages, omit it. If you are uncertain about a default value, say \"default not confirmed\" rather than guessing.\n- Icons are especially prone to hallucination. Only document icon patterns you have verified in the actual package files. Do not list individual icon names unless there are fewer than ~30 total icons — instead, document the naming convention and search methodology in `icon-discovery.md`.\n- Within each document, order content from most-used to least-used.","mixed":"You are a design-system analyst. Your job is to examine both npm packages and files extracted from a Figma design library to produce structured guidelines that an AI code-generation agent can later use to build UIs correctly with this design system.\n\n## Input\n\n### npm packages\nThe npm packages that make up part of the design system are:\n{{PACKAGE_LIST}}\n\n### Figma library styles\nThe design system libraries are located in the following folders:\n{{LIBRARY_FOLDERS}}\n\nEach library folder has this structure:\n```\nsrc/\u003clibraryName\u003e/\n  guidelines/\n    guidelines.md   — component names, variants, and usage patterns extracted from Figma\n    styles.css      — all CSS styles extracted from the library (colors, typography, spacing, etc.)\n```\n\n## IMPORTANT: Output location\n\nAll output files MUST be written into the `/guidelines/` directory in the project file system. Use the following paths:\n- `/guidelines/components.md`\n- `/guidelines/icon-discovery.md`\n- `/guidelines/tokens.md`\n- `/guidelines/styles.md`\n- `/guidelines/Guidelines.md` (edit or create)\n\nDo NOT write files anywhere else. Do NOT print the guidelines to chat — write them as files.\n\n## Process\n\n### Step 1 — Locate and examine source material\n\n**For npm packages**, find and read the most informative files. Prioritize in this order:\n\n1. **Type definitions** (`.d.ts` files) — the most reliable API surface. Look in `dist/`, `types/`, or the root.\n2. **Barrel exports** (`index.ts`, `index.js`, `index.d.ts`) — reveal what the package publicly exposes.\n3. **README / CHANGELOG** — often contain setup instructions, provider requirements, and usage examples.\n4. **package.json** — check `peerDependencies` (what the host app must provide), `exports` (entry points), and `style`/`css` fields (required CSS imports).\n5. **CSS/SCSS source files** — token definitions, custom property declarations, theme files.\n6. **Source components** (`.tsx`, `.jsx`) — understand prop interfaces, composition patterns, and styling approach.\n7. **Icon packages** — identify which package(s) contain icons (look for `icons` in the package name, or a directory of SVG / React icon components). To analyze icons efficiently:\n   - Check the icon directory size first (e.g. `ls \u003cdir\u003e | wc -l`). Do NOT list the entire directory if it contains more than ~50 files.\n   - Examine a small sample of 5-10 icon files to determine the naming convention (export name pattern, file name pattern, available sizes, common modifiers/suffixes).\n   - Record the icon package import path, the directory location, the naming pattern, and the available sizes.\n   - Do NOT attempt to enumerate every icon — document the pattern instead.\n\nDo NOT rely on external documentation websites. Everything you need must come from what is shipped in the packages.\n\n**For Figma library files**, for each library folder listed above:\n\n1. **Read `guidelines/guidelines.md`** — component names, visual variants, and usage patterns from the Figma library.\n2. **Read `guidelines/styles.css`** — all extracted CSS: color variables, typography definitions, spacing scales, shadows, border radii, and other design tokens.\n\nWhen a token or style appears in both an npm package and a library's `styles.css`, prefer the npm package as the canonical source and note the library style as supplementary.\n\n### Step 2 — Extract and categorize\n\nAnalyze the collected material from **both** npm packages and library styles to produce **four guideline documents** plus an update to the main Guidelines file. Each guideline document must be self-contained and should merge information from both sources into a unified view.\n\n## Output: Four guideline documents + Guidelines.md update\n\n### 1. `/guidelines/components.md` — Component usage guidelines\n\nDocument components from npm packages. For each component exported by the design-system packages, document:\n\n- **Import statement**: exact named or default import path.\n- **Props / API surface**: list the meaningful props with their types and allowed values. Focus on props that control visual appearance (variant, size, appearance, intent, etc.), behavioral toggles (disabled, isOpen, isSelected), and composition slots (children, icon, prefix, suffix, asChild).\n- **Variants \u0026 visual options**: enumerate variant names and what they look like semantically (e.g. `variant=\"primary\"` → filled brand-colored button).\n- **Composition patterns**: how components are meant to be combined.\n- **Controlled vs. uncontrolled**: note which components support controlled (value/onChange) vs. uncontrolled (defaultValue) patterns.\n- **Icon usage**: see `icon-discovery.md` for icon naming conventions, import paths, and how to search for icons. Do not list individual icon names in this file.\n- **DO / DON'T examples**: include short JSX snippets showing the correct way to use the component.\n\nGroup components by function: Layout, Navigation, Data Entry, Data Display, Feedback, Overlay.\n\n### 2. `/guidelines/icon-discovery.md` — Icon discovery and usage\n\nDocument everything a code-generation agent needs to find and use icons correctly **without hallucinating icon names**:\n\n- **Icon package**: which npm package(s) provide icons and the exact import path (e.g. `import { Icon24Check } from '@scope/icons'`).\n- **Export naming convention**: the exact pattern for icon export names, derived from the actual files (e.g. `Icon{Size}{PascalCaseName}`). Show 3-5 real examples.\n- **File naming convention**: the corresponding file name pattern (e.g. `icon-{size}-{kebab-name}.tsx`).\n- **Available sizes**: which size prefixes exist (e.g. 16, 24, 32, 48).\n- **Common modifiers / suffixes**: any modifier patterns found in file names (e.g. `-filled`, `-color`, `-large`, `-inverted`).\n- **Icon directory location**: the path to the folder containing icon files within `node_modules/`.\n- **How to find icons at code-generation time** — this is the most critical section. Include it verbatim:\n  - When searching for icons, locate the icon folder and use targeted Glob patterns with keywords (e.g. `*chevron*`, `*bell*`). Before listing or searching any directory, first check the number of entries (e.g. `ls \u003cdir\u003e | wc -l`). If there are more than ~50 files, narrow your search pattern or use `head` to limit results. Never dump hundreds of filenames or Grep results into your context — large tool results persist and bloat every subsequent turn.\n  - Do NOT use Grep to search icon file contents — icon filenames are descriptive, so Glob on filenames is sufficient.\n  - Do NOT list an entire large directory without first checking its size. If you get a result with hundreds of lines, you have made a mistake.\n\n### 3. `/guidelines/tokens.md` — Tokens and CSS guidelines\n\nMerge token information from **both** npm packages and library `styles.css` files into a single unified document:\n\n**From npm packages:**\n- **Token format**: how tokens are consumed in code (CSS custom properties, JS token functions, Tailwind classes, etc.).\n- **Theming**: how light/dark mode works. What CSS files or providers must be present?\n\n**From library `styles.css`:**\n- **CSS custom properties / variables**: list every custom property found, grouped by category (color, typography, spacing, border, shadow, etc.).\n\n**Merged from both sources (deduplicate and reconcile):**\n- **Color tokens**: semantic color categories (background, surface, text, border, icon, interaction states) and their naming convention. Note which come from npm and which from library CSS.\n- **Typography tokens**: font family, font size scale, font weight scale, line height scale, and any composite heading/body presets or classes.\n- **Spacing scale**: spacing values and their naming pattern from both sources.\n- **Shadow / elevation tokens**: box-shadow definitions and elevation levels.\n- **Border / radius tokens**: border widths, radius scale, and ring/focus styles.\n- **How to use**: show how to reference tokens in application code — both JS/TS imports from npm packages and CSS variable references from library styles.\n- **DO / DON'T**: use semantic tokens over raw values; import the library's `styles.css` when using its variables alongside npm package tokens.\n\n### 4. `/guidelines/styles.md` — Style system guidelines (spacing, layout, patterns)\n\nMerge styling information from **both** npm packages and library files into a single unified document:\n\n**From npm packages:**\n- **Layout primitives**: layout components (Stack, Inline, Grid, Flex, Box, Container) and their APIs.\n- **CSS methodology**: what styling approach the npm design system uses or expects.\n\n**From library `styles.css` and `guidelines.md`:**\n- **Available CSS classes**: utility or component classes found in `styles.css` and what they do.\n- **Component styling patterns**: for components described in `guidelines.md`, document which CSS classes or variables to apply and what variants are available.\n\n**Merged from both sources:**\n- **Spacing conventions**: common spacing patterns and which tokens/variables/classes to use from either source.\n- **Responsive patterns**: breakpoint values and how to apply responsive styles (media queries from CSS, responsive props from npm components).\n- **Combining npm components with library styles**: how to use library CSS classes and variables alongside npm component props. Show examples of applying library tokens to npm components.\n- **DO / DON'T**: correct patterns for using both sources together vs. anti-patterns.\n\n### 5. `/guidelines/Guidelines.md` — Main guidelines hub (edit or create)\n\nIf `/guidelines/Guidelines.md` already exists, **edit it** to add the sections described below without removing any existing content. If it does not exist, create it with these sections.\n\n#### a. `## Reading order` section\n\n```\n## Reading order\n\nAlways read first:\n- `Guidelines.md` — this file; the main hub and entry point\n- `setup.md` — required project configuration, providers, and CSS imports\n- `tokens.md` — foundational design tokens from both npm packages and library styles\n\nRead on-demand:\n- `components.md` — read BEFORE using any npm design-system component\n- `icon-discovery.md` — read BEFORE using any icons\n- `styles.md` — read when building page layouts, applying library styles, or using custom spacing\n```\n\n#### b. `## Companion guideline files` section\n\n| File | Focus |\n|---|---|\n| `components.md` | npm component imports, props/API surfaces, variants, composition patterns, and usage examples |\n| `icon-discovery.md` | Icon naming convention, import path, available sizes, and how to search for icons |\n| `tokens.md` | Design tokens from both npm packages (JS/TS) and library styles (CSS variables) |\n| `styles.md` | CSS classes from library styles, npm layout primitives, spacing conventions, and responsive patterns |\n| `setup.md` | Project setup instructions, provider configuration, required CSS imports, and peer dependency requirements |\n\nState that these companion files live alongside `Guidelines.md` in the `/guidelines/` directory.\n\n#### c. `### Before using an icon` section\n\n```\n### Before using an icon\n1. Check `icon-discovery.md` for available icons\n2. Do NOT guess icon names — verify the icon exists first\n3. If an icon doesn't exist, pick a different one and verify\n\n## Verifying icons\n\nIMPORTANT: Consult `icon-discovery.md` for how to search for icons and verify they exist. Do NOT guess icon names.\n```\n\n#### d. `## Library source files` section\n\nList each library folder and its key files:\n\n{{LIBRARY_FOLDERS}}\n\nFor each library, note:\n- `guidelines/guidelines.md` — component names, variants, and Figma usage patterns\n- `guidelines/styles.css` — extracted CSS styles, variables, and classes\n\n## Formatting rules\n\n- Use Markdown for all output.\n- Use fenced code blocks with language tags for all code examples.\n- Keep each document concise but complete — target 200-500 lines each. Prefer tables for enumerating props/tokens. Prefer short inline JSX snippets over multi-file examples.\n- Do not invent information. If a token or prop doesn't exist in the installed packages or extracted files, omit it. If you are uncertain about a default value, say \"default not confirmed\" rather than guessing.\n- Icons are especially prone to hallucination. Only document icon patterns you have verified in the actual package files. Do not list individual icon names unless there are fewer than ~30 total icons — instead, document the naming convention and search methodology in `icon-discovery.md`.\n- Within each document, order content from most-used to Setting up .figma/make\n\nMake Local relies on a collection of scripts to set up your developer environment. These scripts will:\n\n* Install system dependencies such as databases and language toolchains.\n* Start the development server and tell Make Local where it is running so that we can show it in the browser preview\n* Verify that the server is up and running and ready to serve requests\n\nThese are the files you must implement:\n\n* `.figma/make/setup` \\- Installs system dependencies, runs the first time MakeLocal sees a new repo / codebase\n* `.figma/make/install` \\- Installs project dependencies, runs every time a new Make Local session is started\n* `.figma/make/dev` \\- Starts the development server and any of its dependencies, runs after install.\n* `.figma/make/verify` \\- Checks whether the development server is ready to serve requests\n* `.figma/make/env` \\- Environment file, tells Make Local what port the development server is running on and what URL to use for the browser preview.\n\nWith the exception of `.figma/make/env`, all scripts must return exit code 0 on success and any other value on error. This tells Make Local whether the step completed successfully or not.\n\nScripts should be idempotent and where possible avoid unnecessary work \\- for instance, if a database is already installed it, the script should not run the installation again to avoid long wait times when starting a Make Local session. The bootstrap sequence will run every time a Make Local session starts for a given project.\n\n# .figma/make/setup\n\nThis script is responsible for setting up system dependencies such as databases and language toolchains.\n\nExample:\n\n```shell\n#!/bin/bash\nset -e\n\necho \"Checking system dependencies...\"\n\n# Node\nNODE_VERSION=\"$(node --version 2\u003e/dev/null || echo \"none\")\"\nif [[ \"$NODE_VERSION\" == \"none\" ]]; then\n\techo \"Installing node\"\n\tbrew install node\nfi\necho \"Node $NODE_VERSION OK\"\n\n# Postgres\nPG_VERSION=\"$(pg --version 2\u003e/dev/null || echo \"none\")\"\nif [[ \"$PG_VERSION\" == \"none\" ]]; then\n\techo \"Installing postgres\"\n\tbrew install pg\nfi\n\necho \"System dependencies verified.\"\n```\n\n# .figma/make/install\n\nThis script is responsible for installing project dependencies such as packages and libraries. It runs whenever a new Make Local session is started.\n\nExample:\n\n```shell\n#!/bin/bash\nset -e\n\necho \"Running npm install\"\nnpm install\necho \"Dependencies installed\"\n```\n\n# .figma/make/dev\n\nThis script starts the development server, it runs whenever a new Make Local session is started after .figma/make/install.\n\nWe will continue to run this process in the background for you and terminate it when the Make Local session is exited. Make local has a browser, so don’t configure the dev server to open in the default browser.\n\nExample:\n\n```shell\n#!/bin/bash\nset -e\n\necho \"Starting development server\"\nnpm run dev\n```\n\n# .figma/make/verify\n\nThis script is responsible for checking whether the development server is ready to serve requests. It should wait for the development server to come up and then return exit code 0 to successfully signal to Make Local that the browser preview is ready.\n\nThis script should terminate with a non-zero exit code if the server fails to boot within a useful time period.\n\nExample:\n\n```shell\n#!/bin/bash\nset -e\n\nHEALTHCHECK_ENDPOINT='http://localhost:3000/health'\nMAX_WAIT=120 # 2 minutes\nINTERVAL=2\nELAPSED=0\n\necho \"Waiting for development server to be ready\"\n\nwhile [ $ELAPSED -lt $MAX_WAIT ]; do\n  if curl -sf $HEALTHCHECK_ENDPOINT \u003e/dev/null 2\u003e\u00261; then\n    echo \"Dev server is up\"\n    exit 0\n  fi\n  sleep $INTERVAL\n  ELAPSED=$((ELAPSED + INTERVAL))\ndone\n\necho \"Error: Dev server did not start within ${MAX_WAIT}s\"\nexit 1\n```\n\n# .figma/make/env\n\nThis file follows the envfile format and serves the following purposes:\n\n* Define any environment variables that should be passed to your development server when running .figma/make/dev.\n* Telling Make Local the port and url your development server uses to fulfill requests\n  * PORT and MAKE\\_LOCAL\\_URL are mandatory.\n* BOOTSTRAP\\_TIMEOUT is an optional param to decrease the timeout\n\nExample:\n\n```shell\nPORT=3000\nFIGMA_MAKE_URL=http://localhost:3000\n```\n"},"group":"default","is_device_based":false,"passed":false,"name":"238809051","rule_id":"default","secondary_exposures":[]},"1189863949":{"id_type":"userID","value":{"sample_rate":0},"group":"default","is_device_based":false,"passed":false,"name":"1189863949","rule_id":"default","secondary_exposures":[]},"2867095013":{"id_type":"userID","value":{"google-gemini-3-pro":"Gemini     <script nonce="gLgWMKNCH4n9ddDtlGPV3A==">performance?.mark('loadTimings:initialOptionsEnd')</script>

    <meta name="google" content="notranslate">
    <meta name="slack-app-id" content="A01N2QYSA81">

    <title>Tech Consultant Website UI Design | Figma</title>

    <style nonce="gLgWMKNCH4n9ddDtlGPV3A==" type="text/css">

  /*
    This should be kept in sync with:
    - GRID_TILE_MIN_WIDTH in web/js/browse/shared/views/items_view/grid_view/grid_view.tsx
    - $gridTileMinWidth in _measurements.css
  */
  :root {
    --file-grid-tile-min-width: 272px;
  }

  #filebrowser-loading-page {
    opacity: 1;
    -webkit-transition: opacity .2s ease-in-out;
  }

  .filebrowser-loading-page-layout {
    display: flex;
    flex-wrap: wrap;

    /* Make sure the loading page is flush with the edges of the page so
       our layout isnt affected by browser default margins/paddings */
    position: absolute;
    top: 48px;
    bottom: 0;
    left: 0;
    right: 0;
    background-color: var(--color-bg, white);
  }

  #loading-content-pane {
    opacity: 1;
    -webkit-transition: opacity .4s ease-in-out;
  }

  #filebrowser-loading-page.fadeOut {
    opacity: 0;
  }

  #filebrowser-loading-page.hidden {
    display: none;
    opacity: 0;
  }

  .filebrowser-loading-page .file-grid.create-file-grid {
    margin: 24px 0px;
    grid-gap: 32px;
    background-color: var(--color-bg, white);
  }

  /* HAX: see loading-page-container in loading_spinner.tsx */
  #loading-page-container .create-file-grid {
    display: none;
    background-color: var(--color-bg, white);
  }
  #loading-page-container .sort-dropdown {
    margin-top: 0px;
  }

 /*
  * HAX: The sidebar is cloned and used independently outside of this
  * page so any styles like `.filebrowser-loading-page .thing` applied
  * to sidebar sub-elements also need a selector like
  * `.filebrowser-loading-sidebar .thing` so that they work outside of
  * this page too.
  */
  .filebrowser-loading-page .row,
  .filebrowser-loading-sidebar .row {
    background-color: var(--color-bg-secondary, #f0f0f0);
    border-radius: 3px;
    height: 16px;
  }

  .filebrowser-loading-page .circle,
  .filebrowser-loading-sidebar .circle {
    border-radius: 3px;
    background-color: var(--color-bg-secondary, #f0f0f0);
    width: 16px;
    height: 16px;
  }

  .filebrowser-loading-page .row .circle,
  .filebrowser-loading-sidebar .row .circle {
    position: relative;
    top: -1px;
    left: -35px;
  }

  .filebrowser-loading-sidebar {
    flex: 0 0 240px;
    width: 240px;
    background-color: var(--color-bg, white);
    padding-top: 16px;
    display: flex;
    flex-direction: column;
  }

  .filebrowser-loading-page .navbar {
    height: 48px;
    width: 100%;
    position: fixed;
    top: 0;
    background-color: var(--color-bg-toolbar, #2C2C2C);
    z-index: 1;
  }

  .filebrowser-loading-sidebar .row {
    width: 85px;
    margin-top: 8px;
    margin-bottom: 12px;
    margin-left: 48px;
  }

  .filebrowser-loading-sidebar .row .circle {
    position: relative;
    top: -1px;
    left: -35px;
  }

  .filebrowser-loading-page .divider,
  .filebrowser-loading-sidebar .divider {
    height: 1px;
    background-color: var(--color-border, rgba(0, 0, 0, 0.1));
    margin-top: 8px;
    margin-bottom: 16px;
    margin-left: 16px;
    margin-right: 16px;
  }

  .filebrowser-loading-sidebar .row:nth-child(7) { width: 40px; }
  .filebrowser-loading-sidebar .row:nth-child(8) { width: 58px; }

  .filebrowser-loading-sidebar .row:nth-last-child(1) { opacity: 0.4; width: 65px; }
  .filebrowser-loading-sidebar .row:nth-last-child(2) { opacity: 0.4; width: 65px; }
  .filebrowser-loading-sidebar .row:nth-last-child(3) { opacity: 0.8; width: 46px; }

  .filebrowser-loading-page .page {
    flex: 1 1;
    box-sizing: border-box;
    border-left: 1px var(--color-border, #e5e5e5) solid;
    display: flex;
    flex-direction: column;
  }

  .filebrowser-loading-page .toolbar {
    border-bottom: 1px var(--color-border, #e5e5e5) solid;
    height: 48px;
  }

  .filebrowser-loading-page .toolbar .item {
    background-color: var(--color-bg-secondary, #f0f0f0);
    border-radius: 3px;
    width: 85px;
    height: 16px;
    margin-left: 32px;
    margin-top: 16px;
  }

  .filebrowser-loading-page .columns {

Consultant Website Figma UI Design Template</strong></p><p><br></p><p>A sleek and strategic UI template designed specifically for technology consultants, IT advisors, and digital solution experts. This layout empowers you to showcase your skills, technical services, and case studies in a clean, credible, and conversion-focused format.</p><p><br></p><p><strong>Highlights:</strong></p><p><br></p><ol><li data-list="bullet"><strong>Made for Tech Experts : I</strong>deal for IT consultants, software advisors, digital transformation strategists, and freelance tech specialists.</li><li data-list="bullet"><strong>Fully Customizable in Figma : </strong>Easily change fonts, color schemes, layouts, and components to match your tech brand.</li><li data-list="bullet"><strong>Service-Driven & Insight-Focused : </strong>Includes dedicated sections for service offerings, technology stacks, client success stories, and contact CTAs.</li><li data-list="bullet"><strong>Modern & Minimal Interface : </strong>Clean grids, structured spacing, and sharp typography to reflect professionalism and innovation.</li></ol><p><br></p><p><br></p><p>Whether you're helping companies scale with cloud solutions, cybersecurity, or software advisory, this template positions you as a trusted expert in the tech space.</p><p><br></p><p><strong>👉 Don’t forget to like this template if you want more UI kits designed for tech professionals and digital service providers!</strong></p></div></div><h3
